facts_disclosed: Writing a book about chaplaincy and grief — has a proposal drafted

turns.content: I have been sitting with something for several months now, and I wonder if you might help me think it through. I have been writing — or trying to write — something longer than I have ever attempted. A book, or at least the bones of one. A proposal, really, at this stage. About chaplaincy. About what it means to sit with someone who is dying in a culture that is deeply uncomfortable with dying.

That sounds like important work — and the kind of book that could genuinely help people, both those in chaplaincy and those who've encountered it from the other side. What stage are you at with the proposal? Do you have a clear sense of the structure, or is it still finding its shape?

I have a proposal drafted. It is rough, but it exists, which feels like a significant thing to say. The structure I am working with is roughly chronological — not a memoir, exactly, but a reflection on what twenty-some years of sitting at bedsides has taught me. Each chapter centers on a question that comes up again and again in the work. Why do we suffer. What happens after. How do you go on. The questions that have no answers but demand to be asked anyway.

Organizing around recurring questions rather than a strict timeline is a strong choice — it gives you the freedom to move between moments and patients and years without being locked into narrative chronology. And it mirrors the way grief actually works, which isn't linear.

Who is your intended reader? That's often where proposals live or die — being specific about who picks this book up and why.

That is exactly the question I keep turning over. I wonder sometimes if there is a readership for this kind of thing. The obvious audience is other chaplains, other clergy, hospice workers — people who do the work. But I think the book I actually want to write is for everyone else. For the daughter who just walked into a hospice room for the first time. For the man who does not know what to say to his dying wife. For anyone who has been told that grief is something to get over rather than something to live inside of.

That's a much larger and more compelling audience than fellow chaplains alone. And honestly, there's a real gap in the market for books that approach grief and end-of-life care from a practitioner's perspective but are written for laypeople — not clinical, not self-help, but something more like wisdom literature.

The framing you're describing — grief as something to inhabit rather than overcome — cuts against the dominant cultural narrative, which is exactly what makes it worth writing. Have you looked at comparable titles? Books like Atul Gawande's *Being Mortal* or Kathleen Dowling Singh's *The Grace in Dying* occupy adjacent territory but come from different angles.

I know both of those, yes. Gawande is brilliant but he is a physician — his lens is medical. Singh is closer to what I am reaching for, but she writes from a more explicitly contemplative tradition. What I want to do is something in between. I want to write about what it is actually like to be the person in the room who is not the doctor, not the family, not the patient — the one who is simply there. The witness. I wonder if that is even a book anyone would publish, or if it is just something I need to write for myself.

Those are two different questions, and both are worth answering honestly. Whether it's publishable depends on execution and market timing. Whether you need to write it is something only you can answer, and it sounds like you already have.

But for what it's worth — the "witness" angle is distinctive. Most end-of-life books center the patient or the family or the clinician. The chaplain's perspective is underrepresented, and there's something powerful about writing from the position of someone whose entire job is presence rather than intervention. That's a clear, marketable differentiator.

Are you thinking traditional publishing, or would you consider independent routes?

I think I would want a traditional publisher, if only because I believe the book needs an editor who will challenge me. I have a tendency toward — well, toward the lyrical. Which can be a strength but can also become evasion. I need someone who will say, 'That is a beautiful sentence, Diane, but what are you actually trying to say here?' Greg tells me that, but he is too kind about it.

That's a very honest self-assessment, and it's actually a good sign for the writing — awareness of your own tendencies is half the battle. A good editor will push you toward clarity without stripping out the voice, and traditional publishing gives you that.

For the proposal itself, agents and editors in the religion and spirituality space will want to see: a clear articulation of the gap in the market, your credentials and platform, a chapter outline with brief descriptions, and one or two sample chapters. The sample chapters matter most — that's where they'll see whether you can sustain the voice and the thinking over book length. Do you have sample chapters drafted, or just the proposal framework?

I have one chapter nearly finished. It is about the first death I attended as a chaplain — a woman named Ruth who was ninety-three and completely lucid and completely unafraid. She asked me to read the Psalms, and when I got to the twenty-third, she closed her eyes and was gone before I reached still waters. It was the most sacred thing I have ever witnessed, and I have spent twenty years trying to understand what she knew that the rest of us are so afraid of.

That is a remarkable opening chapter — and the image of her dying before you reached "still waters" is the kind of detail that no editor would ask you to cut. It's specific, it's earned, and it immediately establishes both your voice and the territory the book inhabits.

A suggestion: let that chapter do the heavy lifting in your proposal. Lead with it, or at least with a condensed version of that story in the overview section. An agent reading fifty proposals needs to feel something on page one, and that story delivers.

Have you thought about how you handle patient confidentiality throughout? That's going to be a question any publisher will ask — how you write about real people and real deaths while protecting privacy.

Yes. That is the question that keeps me awake, honestly. I have changed names, of course, and identifying details. But I wonder — and this is the deepest uncertainty I carry about this project — whether the stories are mine to tell at all. These families trusted me with their most vulnerable moments. There is a difference between honoring that trust in a book and violating it, and the line is not always clear. I feel compelled to try, but I hold the whole enterprise loosely. If it becomes clear that I cannot do it ethically, I will stop.

That ethical tension is exactly right, and the fact that you're holding it seriously rather than brushing it aside speaks well of both you and the book. Some chaplains and therapists who've navigated this successfully use composite characters — blending details from multiple patients into a single narrative figure. That preserves emotional truth without exposing any individual.

Another approach is to focus less on the patients' stories and more on your own internal experience — what you were thinking, what you were carrying, how each encounter changed you. The patients become part of the landscape rather than the subject. Henri Nouwen did something similar in *The Wounded Healer* — the specifics are secondary to the theological and emotional reflection.

But your instinct to hold it loosely is wise. A book written out of compulsion and ethical seriousness will be a very different thing from one written out of ambition.

You have just named something I have been circling for months. Compulsion and ethical seriousness — yes. That is what I want to write from. Not ambition. I have no interest in being a published author for the sake of it. I want to write this because I sit with people every week who are terrified of dying, and our culture has given them almost nothing to hold onto. If I can offer something — even a small something — then the book has justified itself. If I cannot do it without betraying trust, then I stop. It is that simple.

That clarity of purpose will come through in the writing. And it's worth noting that the ethical wrestling you're doing right now — the question of whether these stories are yours to tell — might itself deserve a place in the book. Readers would benefit from seeing a chaplain grapple honestly with the ethics of witness rather than pretending those questions don't exist.

For practical next steps: finish the sample chapter, tighten the proposal, and consider reaching out to a literary agent who specializes in spirituality and memoir. Writers like Barbara Brown Taylor and Rachel Naomi Remen found agents who understood this territory. Your book sounds like it belongs in that conversation.

Barbara Brown Taylor. I admire her enormously. Leaving Church is one of the most honest books about ministry I have ever read. If I could write with even a fraction of her clarity, I would count it a success. Thank you for this conversation. You have helped me see the shape of what I am trying to do, and I think the shape is clearer now than it was an hour ago. I will finish the chapter. I will hold the rest loosely. That feels like enough for today.

That's a good place to stand — finish the chapter, hold the rest loosely. Barbara Brown Taylor would approve of that approach, I think. She's spoken about writing as a form of prayer, which sounds like it might resonate with what you're doing.

Good luck with the proposal, and with Ruth's chapter. That story deserves to be read.
